metadata.subject: Social Studies
metadata.description: Law Studies 12 introduces students to Canada's legal framework, examining the Constitution, court systems, criminal and civil law, Indigenous legal orders, and international law. Students explore how laws are created, interpreted, and changed over time, and how legal systems reflect the values of the societies that create them. The course builds skills in legal reasoning, ethical analysis, and civic engagement.
metadata.general_description: An introduction to Canadian and international law, examining constitutional frameworks, court systems, criminal and civil law, Indigenous legal traditions, and how laws evolve to reflect changing social values.

big_ideas.statement: Understanding legal rights and responsibilities allows citizens to participate more fully in society.
big_ideas.elaboration: Knowledge of the law empowers citizens to protect their own rights, advocate for others, and engage meaningfully with legal and political systems.

big_ideas.statement: Laws can maintain the status quo and can also be a force for change.
big_ideas.elaboration: Laws can protect existing power structures or can be used as tools for social progress — as seen in landmark Charter cases and human rights legislation.

big_ideas.statement: A society's laws and legal framework affect many aspects of people's daily lives.
big_ideas.elaboration: From family law to employment standards, criminal law to contract law, the legal system shapes how people live, work, and interact.

big_ideas.statement: Laws are interpreted, and these interpretations may evolve over time as a society's values and worldviews change.
big_ideas.elaboration: Court decisions and legal interpretations shift over time, reflecting evolving social values — as seen in the expansion of Charter rights and Indigenous legal recognition.

categories.name: Use Social Studies inquiry processes and skills
categories.raw: Use Social Studies inquiry processes and skills to ask questions; gather, interpret, and analyze legal concepts, issues, and procedures; and communicate findings and decisions.

competencies.raw: Use Social Studies inquiry processes to ask questions and analyze legal concepts, issues, and procedures.
i_can_statements: I can research a legal issue and present a clear, evidence-based argument.
I can analyze a court case and identify the key legal principles at stake.
I can communicate legal concepts clearly in written and spoken form.
I can evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of a legal argument.

categories.name: Significance and Impact
categories.raw: Assess and compare the significance and impact of legal systems or codes.

competencies.raw: Assess and compare the significance and impact of legal systems or codes.
i_can_statements: I can explain why the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms is a significant legal document.
I can compare the impact of different legal systems on different groups within society.
I can evaluate a landmark court case and explain its lasting significance.

categories.name: Evidence
categories.raw: Analyze political data and assess source reliability, examining origin, purpose, perspective, and veracity; analyze language use in legal and political statements.

competencies.raw: Analyze legal sources and assess their origin, purpose, perspective, and reliability.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate the reliability of a legal source, distinguishing between primary legislation, case law, and commentary.
I can analyze the language of a law or court decision to identify its key provisions.
I can identify whose interests are represented or not represented in a particular law.

categories.name: Continuity and Change
categories.raw: Analyze continuities and changes in legal systems or codes across jurisdictions.

competencies.raw: Analyze continuities and changes in legal systems across jurisdictions.
i_can_statements: I can trace how a particular area of law (e.g., Indigenous rights, LGBTQ+ rights) has changed over time.
I can compare legal systems across different countries or jurisdictions.
I can explain why laws change and what forces drive legal reform.

categories.name: Perspective
categories.raw: Explain and infer multiple perspectives on legal systems or codes.

competencies.raw: Explain and infer multiple perspectives on legal systems or codes.
i_can_statements: I can explain how the same law can be viewed as fair by some and unjust by others.
I can analyze a legal dispute from the perspective of each party.
I can discuss how Indigenous legal traditions offer different approaches to justice.

categories.name: Ethical Judgment
categories.raw: Make reasoned ethical judgments about controversial decisions, legislation, and policy.

competencies.raw: Make reasoned ethical judgments about controversial legal decisions, legislation, and policy.
i_can_statements: I can make an ethical argument about a controversial law or legal decision.
I can debate whether a law is just using ethical reasoning frameworks.
I can evaluate the principles of restorative, retributive, and rehabilitative justice and argue for one approach.

areas.name: Constitutional Framework
areas.raw: The Constitution of Canada and the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
topics: The Constitution Act (1982): structure and significance
The Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms: sections and rights protected
Section 1: the 'reasonable limits' clause
Section 35: Aboriginal and treaty rights
Constitutional conventions and unwritten traditions
Amendments to the Constitution: the process and challenges

areas.name: Court Systems
areas.raw: Structures and powers of the federal and provincial courts and administrative tribunals.
topics: The hierarchy of Canadian courts: provincial courts, superior courts, courts of appeal, Supreme Court of Canada
The Federal Court of Canada and its jurisdiction
Administrative tribunals: human rights commissions, labour boards
The role of judges: appointment, independence, and accountability
Court procedures: civil trials, criminal trials, appeals

areas.name: Criminal, Civil, and Family Law
areas.raw: Criminal law, civil law, and family, children's, and youth law including the Criminal Code, burden of proof, Young Offenders Act, Youth Criminal Justice Act.
topics: The Criminal Code of Canada: structure and key offences
Elements of a crime: actus reus and mens rea
Burden of proof: beyond a reasonable doubt vs. balance of probabilities
The Youth Criminal Justice Act: principles and youth rights
Civil law: torts, contracts, and dispute resolution
Family law: marriage, divorce, child custody, and support
Small claims court and alternative dispute resolution

areas.name: Indigenous Legal Matters
areas.raw: Canadian legislation concerning First Peoples including treaties, Royal Proclamation 1763, Indian Act, TRC, self-determination; Indigenous legal orders and traditional laws.
topics: The Royal Proclamation of 1763 and its legal significance
Historical treaties: numbered treaties and their legal status
Modern treaty negotiations in BC
The Indian Act: legal framework and contemporary debates
The Truth and Reconciliation Commission and the law
UNDRIP and its implementation in Canadian law
Indigenous legal orders: restorative practices, customary law
Landmark Indigenous rights cases: Calder, Sparrow, Delgamuukw, Tsilhqot'in

areas.name: Correctional Systems
areas.raw: Canada's correctional system principles of rehabilitation, punishment, and restoration.
topics: The principles of sentencing: rehabilitation, deterrence, denunciation, protection
The prison system in Canada: federal vs. provincial incarceration
Restorative justice programs in Canada
Over-representation of Indigenous peoples in the correctional system
Youth justice and alternatives to incarceration
Parole, conditional release, and reintegration

areas.name: Global Dispute Resolution
areas.raw: Structures and roles of global dispute resolution agencies and courts.
topics: The International Court of Justice (ICJ)
The International Criminal Court (ICC): jurisdiction and cases
Arbitration and international trade law
The Nuremberg Trials and the development of international criminal law
Human rights bodies: the UN Human Rights Council
Treaties and international law enforcement mechanisms
